author: @gad5248
text:
Just came across this video,
And I'd like to make a couple observations that hopefully other viewers can comment on.
1. Does the Gallium/Aluminum alloy produce pure Hydrogen? Or HHO?
If produces HHO, how do you separate out the O2?
Hydrogen is 0.5 Angstrom and Oxygen is 1.4 Angstrom.
2. Scrap Aluminum at the scrap yard is reasonably cheap, however Aluminum is made with various alloying materials some of which can contaminate the reator. Certainly from a scrap yard it's going to be a collection of all types, how do you pick the right scrap?
Just some thoughts to spark a discussion.

author: @JAllenKaiser
text:
I think the more significant application with the 3:1 Ga:Al mixture isn’t the reaction byproduct of hydrogen, but the one of nanoparticle Al2O3. The Bayer process for making alumina contaminates the batch with sodium which is extremely difficult to completely wash back out…and even a minuscule amount of sodium contamination is a huge problem in alumina-based ceramics, where it acts as a glass-forming flux. Bayer alumina also requires a lot of heat input to dissolve each batch into the mother liquor and then flocculate the alumina from sodium aluminate, and the resultant alumina is an aluminum oxyhydroxide…which then has to have further high-heat input to calcine it into aluminum oxide. On top of all this, the particle sizes tend to be quite large and they agglomerate, which is not very useful for making mesoporous alumina active catalysts, thin films, molecular sieves, zeolites, bio-active microstructures, etc.
Just about every other method of producing low- or no- sodium alumina relies on the use of some other toxic or destructive salt that gets released into the atmosphere or kiln walls during the calcination step (clorides, fluorides, or nitric, sulphuric, or boric acids, etc.) which otherwise makes low-soda alumina both very expensive and very environmentally destructive.
…The gallium route, though: produces aluminum oxide directly, which is free from the typical sodium contamination, is of sub-micron nanometer-sized particle scale, and is already dehydroxylated — so you don’t have to calcine it. Plus, the gallium is recyclable over and over indefinitely. The entire process happens at ambient temp with no additional energy input. That’s super-exciting for ceramicists and chemists working on alumina catalysts for all kinds of alternative energy production and storage, biotech, and chemical engineering, even including selective atmospheric carbon sequestration.

author: @antifreeze-30degrees49
text: You can use Gallium Indium Tin alloy (Or Galinstan) to amalgamate aluminum. Galinstan Aluminum Amalgam produces hydrogen. You can use more and more aluminum to make more Hydrogen. Galinstan is cheaper than pure Gallium and has a lower melting point too. The only problem is that there will be waste Aluminum hydroxide. But we have loads of Aluminum and water vapor doesn't harm the environment and comes back as rain. I don't know what could bring down Aluminum recovery costs. Aluminum Galinstan amalgam could be good for chemical synthesis such as reductions and reductive amination and is a non-toxic alternative to Aluminum mercury.

author: @artytomparis
text:
So I got some gallium to see if all these sites claiming it is a great producer of hydrogen might be onto something. Always question the science. 
Conclusion: It sucks really bad. It's expensive. It's hit and miss. You have to coax it into action. 

Then I tried another method. Much simpler and extremely inexpensive. Lye plus water, plus aluminium. Very simple. It starts to give off hydrogen within seconds and it burns cleanly.

author: @jeffharp4644
text: On a different video I commented on the dangers of using hydrogen as a fuel.  What you get when you burn hydrogen is steam which will greatly increase the amount of water in the atmosphere, which is many times more effective than carbon dioxide in increasing global warming.  Also, as things are we have severe water shortages worldwide and breaking down water for fuel will only exacerbate that problem.  It is possible that we could figure out a way to balance pulling the water from the atmosphere to produce hydrogen with the water going back into the atmosphere from the steam produced from hydrogen engines.  However, that would require strong regulation on how much hydrogen each person would be allowed to burn.  As I said in that other comment, burning hydrogen as a fuel is a very bad idea.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @carlfns8578

author: @YoutubeSupportServices
text:
Yes, I do thoroughly enjoy making water-glass(sodium-silicate) with sodium-hydroxide in a large stainless-steel crock-pot, outside in my multi-year-Engineered, Scientific("That wasn't suppose to happen")-Experimental-Containment, DIRTanium-lined, precautionary, Research-Making-hole... ...THAT I must say; "Through the process of "DAMIT-Calcified-impervious layering", it typically works pretty good!
  EVEN in situations that unexpectedly evolve from benign-certainty to "HOLY-SHI%!, MY EYEBROWS!....DAMIT!".... aaaannnd another layer is added to further increase safety!!
 Because "SAFETY-FIRST!" kids! ...

 Remember: Continuous "Safety-upgrades" are important! First-Hand knowledge is priceless! ...And hair grows back!(But NOT finger-tips!)

 As an example of how important it is to have "Saftey Protocols" standing-by and ready:
 
 IF you're going to make some "Water-Glass", ...ANNNDDDD After the awesomeness of the +100-celcius, extremely vigorous rapid-boiling of unexpected water-based "certainty" evolving into a state of "Scientific-Discovery", that CAN manifest from what's later discovered; "WAS NOT" radiated-heat derived from any newly formed, volcanic activity directly beneath my yard, inadvertently-triggered by a pot of accidentally-discovered magma-pheromones after a little "Firey-Hot-Chip-Powder" contamination,  NOR was it any angry, reawakened spirits, accidentally reawakened with incantations of beer & Doritos that had now bewitched my $25.00usd, Ebay-sourced,  "Ancient Witches-Cauldron"  that had been carelessly mislabeled as a simple "Used Stainless Crock-Pot"... No, unfortunately to my shockingly-devastating disappointment it was merely only a combination of: 
1.) The "Ebay-Seller" of said "crock-pot" forgetting to include the "Stainless-steel part" before packing and shipping her ALUMINUM, crack-pot, crock-pot.
and
2.) The complacent, impatient-nature, of my Doritos-induced Neuropraxial-Retardification....

And that's why I say;.. "THANK GOD I still had the safety of my beer-protocols left to solve everything!

author: @floridacoder
text:
When you use a scour pad in an aluminum container filled with water, why is there no apparent reaction?  Wouldn't you be removing the oxidized layer from that mechanical process?  

Pretty sure if it did react people would have noticed.   Or perhaps the reaction is so subtle and fast (before it re-oxydizes) you can't see with the naked eye?

replies.author: @adrianelliott5555
replies.text: It is because it only creates another oxide layer and prevents the water being exposed to the aluminum. The difference is the gallium disrupts the oxide layer and allows the reaction to continue.

replies.author: @floridacoder
replies.text: @adrianelliott5555 the video says that it takes "seconds" to reform that oxide layer.  Wouldn't scrubbing fast prevent it from reforming in time?

replies.author: @adrianelliott5555
replies.text: @floridacoder yes basically if you continuously sanded the aluminum to remove the oxide layer you could generate hydrogen and aluminium oxide but you would consume far more energy than it would generate.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: aluminium oxide is pretty tough and well stuck to the surface of the Ali mate and the re-oxidation reaction is nano seconds - basically you don't stand a chance of doing it by hand

author: @kevinleebailey
text:
Wood ash (potassium hydroxide) and aluminium in rain water works well.
It's a cheap way to make lighter than air balloons. 
This mixture in a biogas generator instead of bio materials should work well. The real problem is how to recycle the waste.
Just remember that this gas is very explosive when mixed with air and Ignited. Also Potassium hydroxide is very alkaline and corrosive and very harmful to your eyes. Don't breath any vapours from this mixture !
Wood ash + strip's of aluminium pie trays and copper or carbon make simple batteries.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @markwarburton8563
text: Yes, the recovery angle is the sticky bit. With Hydrogen power, there always seems to be a sticky bit. When we finally solve the cradle to grave issues around efficiency, pollution and real world re-use, Hydrogen will be a winner. By then, though, we might all be fully converted to battery power anyway. Hydrogen power is coming second in this race and there's nothing on the horizon at present suggesting it can get a leg up and beat battery power, especially as battery power seems to be a lot more innovative recently.

author: @floridacoder
text: Do you need oxygen for aluminum to oxidize?  If so, what happens under a vacuum where air is removed?  Also is there a mechanical solution as opposed to a chemical one?  Also when reacting with water is oxygen also released in addition to the hydrogen?

replies.author: @Chris47368
replies.text: The reaction is practically identical to that of when alkali metals react with water, the aluminium reacts with the water itself to release hydrogen gas and aluminium hydroxide - as opposed to oxide formed when reacted with oxygen.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes it needs oxygen which it get from the water and that is what releases the hydrogen - but the product is aluminium hydroxide - a mechanical solution is possible in theory but extra-ordinarily difficult to do in practice

replies.author: @Chris47368
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Hey there! Its pretty cool to see you interacting with your audience! I have been watching you since your early experiments involving graphene oxide/graphene super capacitors, keep up the good work!

author: @SeanBZA
text: Yes, all aluminium smelters are in places with cheap electrical power, so Canada by the Niagara Falls, Iceland with geothermal power, South Africa with low cost coal and China, with low cost coal and hydro power. None of them are near the mines, so all of the ore is shipped there to be smelted. the aluminium battery is great in principle, but the cost of recharging it, seeing as your hydroxide is not exactly suitable for the smelter straight off, and you will have a pretty big loss in this cycle.  Then you get less than a quarter of the energy that went into the refinery back making hydrogen, and then further whatever you use to convert it to power will be at best 30% efficient. Probably will be less pollution to simply take the coal, turn it into a fine powder powder, mix with water, and burn in an engine in that case, with at least getting more energy out of the fuel.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice analysis mate - cheers

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
What is the value of gallium and aluminum  oxides or hydroxides after the fuel is spent?  That would offset the cost of the fuel. 

Assuming a reasonable value for the left over products, and putting energy for transportation aside, how about aluminum gallium for home heating?  Could that a viable method for seasonal energy storage considering net cost and volume needs. The exhaust of burning hydrogen is steam that would condense giving off heat in a heat exchanger. The condensed water than can be used to make more hydrogen from the aluminum gallium. 



I strongly suspect that if there becomes a market for aluminum  gallium, that limited supplies of gallium will significantly limit the size of that market. It is a good thing that that aluminum gallium  for energy is fully recyclable. 

Maybe a liquid aluminum sodium potasium alloy might work.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: to be honest mate - the aluminium gallium is an awesome idea but I think it won't work out in a practical way - just too many hurdles

author: @kkrolik2106
text:
I considering now heating my small house via this exothermic reaction due cheap aluminium scrap available nearby CNC shop.

this 3 times cheaper compared to LPG gas heating and 5 times to electric heating.

Reaction 4AL+GA+4H20 => 4AL(OH)3 +3H2+ GA

Gallium is fully recoverable.

All by product are non toxic AL(OH) is even use in meds and toothpaste.

Some numbers

1kw = 3,6MJ

1kg aluminium to AL0H generate 31MJ

10kg aluminium to AL0H generate 310MJ

By-product is Hydrogen 3.9M^3 H2 when burn generate 45MJ

310 + 45 = 355MJ

14.26KG aluminium 442,15Mj

Create 5,56M^3 H2 to 64.18Mj

442.15 + 64.18 = 506,34Mj

This equal Single kitchen 11kg LPG bottle.

11Kg LPG 506,33MJ

Last winter I used single bottle to heat up for 21days.

I will need 0.679KG Aluminium scrap per day to heat up my small house in winter.

I know that hydrogen is dangerous but per day I will not create it to much and I intend burnt it on spot.

Any ideas, opinions ?
What to do with by product like AL(OH)  ?
